title: Chapter 11: The Golden Boy
content: They called it a visit.

The word arrived in my father's email three days before — *Julian Linton will be visiting Blackwood on the 14th for a formal introduction. Please ensure Catalina is available from 4 PM.* — and it sat in my inbox like a subpoena. A visit. As if Julian were a curriculum reviewer popping by to assess the facilities. As if I were the facilities.

I stood on the front steps of the main hall at ten minutes to four, dressed in the cashmere cardigan my father's assistant had couriered, the one that made me look like a country club afterthought. Soft cream. Mother-of-pearl buttons. Exactly the kind of thing a future Linton would wear. I'd studied the Linton women's social media for two days — their aesthetic was old money pretending not to be performance, which was, of course, the most demanding performance of all.

The black town car rolled up the drive at exactly four. Punctual. The Lintons were famous for it; Julian's grandfather had once pulled funding from a merger because the other party was eleven minutes late. Punctuality was Linton code for *we respect the transaction*.
The driver opened the rear door, and Julian Linton unfolded himself from the back seat.

The first thing I noticed was the hair. Golden curls, a shade that caught the late-afternoon sun and held it like it belonged there. Not the over-styled, product-heavy ringlets of boys who spent time on their appearance — these were careless, swept back from his face as if he'd run his hand through them and forgotten about it. The second thing: his height. He was tall enough that he had to duck slightly exiting the car, and when he straightened, he did it with an easy looseness that said his body had never been a source of anxiety. People who moved like that had never been cornered in a room. Had never had to make themselves small.
The third thing was his smile.

It hit me before I could prepare for it. Warm, unhurried, the kind of smile that seemed to say *I'm already glad I met you and we haven't even spoken yet.* I'd read his file cover to cover — 3.94 GPA, trusteeship structure that vested at twenty-five, family portfolio diversified across real estate and biotech, one younger sister at Andover, history of community service that read less like résumé-padding and more like genuine interest. I knew his tax brackets and his blood type.
I didn't know what his smile would do to the air between us.
It made it thinner.

My father appeared beside me on the steps, his hand settling on my shoulder with the practiced weight of a man who owned what he touched. "Julian," Robert said, and his voice carried that particular warmth he reserved for people with more money than him — a warmth that was really hunger dressed in tweed. "Welcome to Blackwood."

"Mr. Vance." Julian crossed the remaining distance with long strides, extending his hand. My father took it. Two men performing the oldest negotiation in human history — the transfer of a woman, made palatable by geography and a generation of polite fiction. "Thank you for having me. The campus is remarkable."

"We're proud of it." My father's fingers tightened once on my shoulder — *your turn* — and then dropped away. "I believe you've seen photographs of Catalina, but photographs rarely do justice. Cat, this is Julian Linton. Julian, my daughter."

Julian turned to me, and I watched his expression shift. The generic pleasantness of greeting an adult male faded, and something more focused took its place. Interest. Genuine, unguarded interest, the kind that hadn't been coached by family advisors or prepped by his father's lawyers. He looked at me like I was a question he wanted to answer.

He took my hand. His palm was warm and dry, his grip firm but not performative — he wasn't trying to impress me with how strong he was, or how gentle. He was simply holding my hand because that was what you did when you met someone, and he was someone who did things because they felt right rather than because they'd been calculated.
"You're even more beautiful than your file suggested," he said.
And my performance began.

I let my smile grow — not too fast, not too bright. A small dilation of pleasure, as if his words had caught me off guard. I tilted my chin down half an inch, letting my lashes do the work my father's etiquette coach had drilled into me at twelve. "You read my file?" I asked, injecting just enough self-deprecation to make the question charming rather than insecure.
"Cover to cover." His thumb brushed the back of my knuckles — a small thing, absent, probably unconscious — and then he released my hand. "Your father is very proud of you."
"My father is very proud of the Linton account."

The words came out sharper than I'd intended, and I watched Julian's expression flicker — surprise, then amusement, then something almost like relief. As if he'd been afraid I'd be a mannequin, and was glad to find a pulse.

"*Cat*," my father said, the single syllable carrying a universe of warning. But Julian laughed. A real laugh, warm and low, the kind that resonated in the chest. It smelled like cedar and something sweeter underneath — his cologne, carried on the October air.
"I think I'm going to like you," Julian said.
I smiled back, and the calculation hummed beneath my skin like a second pulse. *He likes honesty. Note that. File under: access points.*
The afternoon was a performance, and I was brilliant.

I walked Julian through the east gardens, letting him point out the stonework he recognized from his own family's estate in Connecticut. I asked about his sister — *she's thirteen and absolutely terrifying, I adore her* — and laughed when he imitated her eye-roll. I asked about his courses at Columbia, and when he told me he was studying environmental policy, I let genuine curiosity bleed through the performance, because real curiosity was more convincing than manufactured interest, and environmental policy was actually interesting.

I laughed at the right moments. I tilted my head when he spoke, showing the curve of my neck, tracking the way his gaze followed the movement and then politely retreated. I made myself smaller in his presence — not physically, but emotionally, creating space for him to fill, space where he could feel generous and protective and all the things a golden boy needed to feel.
It was perfect.
It was exhausting.

The worst part was that I could see it working in real time. Every soft smile I manufactured, he absorbed and reflected back, amplified. Every careful question I asked, he answered with genuine enthusiasm, and his enthusiasm made the conversation feel real, and the feeling of realness was itself a technique — I knew that, I'd read the literature on rapport-building — but it *felt* like something else, and the split was making me dizzy.

Two calculations ran simultaneously. One: *he's falling, the metrics are good, by the end of this visit he'll be invested enough that the engagement proceeds on schedule.* Two: *I am making him fall, and the mechanism is working, and I am watching myself make it work, and I cannot stop watching, and I cannot stop making it work.*

We stopped on the stone bridge over the ornamental creek. The last light caught the water and broke it into shards of copper and rose. Julian leaned against the railing, and the wind lifted his golden curls, and for a moment he looked like something out of a painting — the privileged son against the privileged backdrop, everything gilded and certain.
"Can I tell you something?" he asked.
"Of course."

"I was dreading this." He said it simply, without drama, as if he were reporting the weather. "The whole drive up. I kept thinking — she's going to hate me. She's going to look at me and see the arrangement, not the person. And I wouldn't even blame her, because that's what I'd see too, if I were in your position."
I waited. Silence was often more useful than response.

"But you're not what I expected," he continued. He turned to face me, and the sunset caught the edges of his face, gilding the same way it gilded the water. "You're smart. You're funny. You're — and I mean this as a compliment, even though it probably sounds like a criticism — *sharper* than anyone in my family gave you credit for."
"I'll take it as a compliment."
"Good." He smiled, and the warmth in it made something in my sternum shift, a small internal click, like a lock turning. "Because you don't have to impress me, Cat. I'm already impressed."
Something in my chest hiccupped.

A small, undignified spasm, the kind of reflex I'd trained out of myself years ago. I filed it immediately — *response to perceived sincerity; flag as vulnerability* — and moved on, smiling, thanking him, steering the conversation toward safer ground.

But the file sat open in my mind, and the label *vulnerability* didn't quite fit. A vulnerability was something an opponent could exploit. This felt like something *I* could exploit — in myself. The temptation of softness. I'd never been allowed softness. Softness was a luxury, and in the Vance family, luxuries were reserved for people who could afford to lose them.

Julian held the door to the dining hall open for me, and I walked through it, and the warmth of his palm brushed the small of my back — light, respectful, appropriate — and I catalogued the touch and moved on.
I was wrong about the label. It wasn't a vulnerability. It was a warning.

Dinner was oysters and Wagyu and a 2018 Puligny-Montrachet that my father had selected specifically because the Lintons kept cases of it at their Martha's Vineyard house. Robert performed beautifully — avuncular, warm, the doting father of a daughter he was only reluctantly parting with. I recognized every beat. Julian, I suspected, did not.

Julian asked about my classes and listened to the answers. He asked about my favorite books and remembered the titles. He looked at me like I was a person instead of a chess piece, and every time he did, I had to remind myself that I was the one playing chess.

After dinner, he walked me back to the dormitory. The October air had turned cold, biting through the cashmere, and I let my arms cross over my chest — a gesture of vulnerability that was also, genuinely, a response to the temperature. The best performances always contained truth.

We stopped at the entrance. The old iron lantern above the door cast a warm amber circle, and Julian stood in it like a painting of a painting — golden boy, golden light, everything in its proper place.
He didn't try to kiss me.

He didn't lean in, didn't reach for my hand, didn't do any of the things I'd been bracing for. He simply looked at me, and his expression was open and serious and so completely without agenda that it almost hurt to witness.
"I know this isn't how either of us would have chosen," he said. "But I want you to know — I'm going to try to make this good. For both of us."
His sincerity was a weapon, and he didn't even know he was holding it.

I nodded. Said goodnight. The words came out steady and soft, exactly as they should. I closed the door, and the latch clicked shut, and I pressed my forehead against the wood and stayed there, breathing, counting the seconds until his footsteps faded down the hall.
I wasn't shaken by his kindness.
I was shaken by how much I wished it were genuine — and how much I wished I were the kind of person who could accept it if it were.

The war ledger was a black Moleskine I kept in the hollowed spine of a philosophy text no one would ever open. I sat at my desk, the dorm room dark except for the thin blade of lamplight falling across the page, and I wrote in my smallest, most precise hand:
*Linton, Julian.*
*Assets: genuine warmth, family financial access, trust fund liquid by 25.*
*Vulnerabilities: believes in love, uncomfortable with cruelty, will not see manipulation in someone he trusts.*
I paused. The pen hovered. The clock on the windowsill ticked, and the sound was very loud in the empty room.
Then I added:
*Vulnerability (mine): wanted him to mean it.*
I looked at the line. Then I drew the pen through it — a single, decisive strike.
I stared at the crossed-out words.
Then I wrote them again, underneath. Slower this time. The ink slightly darker where I'd pressed harder.
*Vulnerability (mine): wanted him to mean it.*
I closed the notebook. Put it back inside the philosophy text. Placed the text on the shelf between Kant and Kierkegaard, where despair belonged.

The wind moved through the old windows, finding the gaps in the frame, whispering against the glass like something trying to get in. I lay in bed with the lights off and my eyes open, watching the ceiling, not seeing it.

I thought about the chapel. About Heath's mouth and the way he'd said my name like it was a sentence he was still deciding on. About the fracture in our pact — the thing that had cracked open between us and let the cold in, and the heat, and the terrifying possibility that the pact had been a cage we'd built for ourselves and the walls were coming down.
I thought about Julian's hand on the small of my back. Light. Respectful. Appropriate. The touch of a man who was trying to be careful with something he valued.

And I thought about how Heath had touched me in the conservatory — hard, possessive, his fingers pressing into the curve of my waist like he was trying to leave a mark, like he needed me to feel the shape of his hand after he'd gone. Real. Ungentle. A touch that said *you are mine and I am yours and we are both going to suffer for it.*
Julian's touch said *I will take care of you.*
Heath's touch said *I will break you open and climb inside.*
I closed my eyes.
I was starting to feel the difference between being held gently and being held like you might break each other.
One was safe.
The other was real.
